summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that micro-ISV founders waste enormous time worrying about things that don't matter for their business success. He systematically dismantles common fears: license terms nobody reads, competitors stealing code, tax complications, and programming language choice. The core argument is that 90% of a software business is marketing and sales, not development, and founders should allocate their worry accordingly. He grounds each point in practical reality checks — comparing software licensing to buying hamburgers, and tax fears to eBay sellers. The post is aimed at solo developers who over-index on technical and legal concerns while under-investing in what actually drives revenue.
key_points: License terms are irrelevant for consumer software because no one reads them and enforcing them costs more than they're worth
Code protection and competitor fears are overblown because 90% of a software business happens outside the IDE, making code theft impractical
Tax fears are irrational for small operators — governments provide free resources and aren't trying to entrap micro-businesses
Programming language choice doesn't matter because development time is a tiny fraction of total business effort
The market in any viable niche is far larger than a solo operator can absorb, so competitor-watching is wasted energy
money_quotes: Here is the thing about babies: while most people absolutely love their baby they've got negative a billion desire to have anyone else's baby.
10% of this business, on the outside, happens in the IDE. The other 90% is where you make your money.
Do you know what the terms and conditions for your hamburgers are? No, because there are none.
In my entire professional career I have never seen debating compile successfully.
Why on earth would you go ahead and consult a lawyer about your license terms — might as well take $1,000 in small bills and burn it as incense.
Your customers lack these, which is why they want to pay you to make their problem go away.

After you have significant revenues, I think hiring a professional to do your taxes and/or your accounting is a good use of money, because the alternative is a poor use of time, and you ideally have a lot of the former but little of the latter.
key_insight: Solo software founders squander their scarcest resource — time — on technical and legal anxieties that have near-zero impact on revenue, when they should be focused on marketing and selling to customers.
